feedback: Initial design for Kaggle Playground Series S6E2 Heart Disease binary classification (AUC metric). Dataset: 630K train rows, 270K test rows, 13 features (mix of numeric and low-cardinality categorical), binary target with ~45% positive rate, no missing values. Hardware: 2x A100 80GB GPUs, 251GB RAM, 96 CPUs. All key libraries are available: pytabkit (RealMLP), xgboost 3.1.3, lightgbm 4.6.0, catboost 1.2.10, tabm, torch 2.8.0, sklearn 1.8.0. sklearn.preprocessing.TargetEncoder is available (cuML is not). Three candidates proposed spanning lightweight GBDTs, medium-weight RealMLP with knowledge distillation, and heavier TabM neural ensemble. All candidates share a common feature engineering pipeline (digit extraction, binning, categorical copies, target encoding) proven critical by top solutions. The 1st place score is AUC 0.95535. All candidates save OOF predictions for cross-candidate ensembling via ridge regression on rank-transformed predictions.

0.name: GBDT Trio Ensemble (XGBoost + LightGBM + CatBoost)
0.architecture: Three gradient boosted decision tree models trained independently: XGBoost (gpu_hist backend on A100), LightGBM (GPU learner), and CatBoost (GPU training with native categorical support). Each model produces OOF predictions and test predictions. The three sets of predictions are blended via a rank-transformed ridge regression meta-learner. XGBoost and LightGBM operate on fully numeric features (with target-encoded categoricals), while CatBoost additionally receives raw categorical features natively.
0.data_pipeline: 1) Load train.csv and test.csv. Map target: Absence=0, Presence=1. Drop 'id' column from features. 2) DIGIT FEATURES: For each numeric column (Age, BP, Cholesterol, Max HR, ST depression), extract individual integer digits as separate features. E.g., Age=58 produces digit_pos_1=8 (ones), digit_pos_2=5 (tens). For ST depression (float), also extract decimal digits: dec_1, dec_2. This captures synthetic data generation artifacts. 3) BINNING FEATURES: For Age, BP, Cholesterol, Max HR, ST depression, create quantile bins (q=4,5,10,20) and uniform bins (bins=5,10) as new integer features. Also create Age rounded to nearest 5. 4) CATEGORICAL COPIES: Convert every original numeric feature to string type to create a categorical version. 5) TARGET ENCODING: Using sklearn.preprocessing.TargetEncoder, encode all categorical features (both original categorical and categorical copies) within each CV fold to prevent leakage. Use fit on training fold, transform on validation fold, and fit on full training data for test set encoding. 6) For CatBoost: additionally pass raw categorical columns (Sex, Chest pain type, FBS over 120, EKG results, Exercise angina, Slope of ST, Number of vessels fluro, Thallium and all binned features) via cat_features parameter. 7) No normalization needed for tree models.
0.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy (logloss) for all three GBDT models. The dataset is reasonably balanced (~45% positive), so no class weighting or oversampling is needed. The evaluation metric is AUC, but logloss as the training objective works well for AUC optimization with GBDTs. Optionally set eval_metric='auc' for early stopping monitoring.
0.regularization: XGBoost: tune max_depth, min_child_weight, subsample, colsample_bytree, reg_alpha (L1), reg_lambda (L2), gamma. LightGBM: tune num_leaves, min_data_in_leaf, feature_fraction, bagging_fraction, lambda_l1, lambda_l2, path_smooth. CatBoost: tune depth, l2_leaf_reg, random_strength, bagging_temperature. All models use early stopping based on validation AUC with a patience parameter. The large dataset (630K rows) provides natural regularization; avoid overly deep trees.
0.training_config: XGBoost: tree_method='hist' with device='cuda', objective='binary:logistic', eval_metric='auc', use early stopping with a patience window. LightGBM: device='gpu', objective='binary', metric='auc', early stopping rounds set via callbacks. CatBoost: task_type='GPU', loss_function='Logloss', eval_metric='AUC', use_best_model=True. All three models train with a tuned number of boosting rounds (set a high max, rely on early stopping). For the meta-learner: rank-transform each model's OOF predictions (scipy.stats.rankdata, normalized to [0,1]), then fit sklearn Ridge regression on the rank-transformed OOFs. Apply same rank transform to test predictions before blending. Train each GBDT model with multiple random seeds (at least 3) and average predictions for stability.
0.validation_strategy: StratifiedKFold cross-validation. Primary metric: AUC (sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score). For each fold, save OOF predictions and fold-specific test predictions. Final test predictions are the mean of fold-specific predictions. Track per-fold AUC and mean +/- std across folds. Save all OOF arrays to disk for cross-candidate ensembling. Also monitor logloss as a secondary metric.
method_steps: Load and preprocess data: read CSVs, map target to 0/1, separate features and target
Implement digit extraction function: for each numeric column, extract integer position digits and decimal digits as new features. Apply to both train and test.

Implement binning features: quantile bins (q=4,5,10,20) and uniform bins (bins=5,10) for Age, BP, Cholesterol, Max HR, ST depression. Also Age rounded to nearest 5. Apply to both train and test consistently (fit bin edges on train, apply to test).
Create categorical copies of all original numeric features by converting to string type
Set up StratifiedKFold CV loop
Within each fold: fit sklearn TargetEncoder on train fold categorical features, transform train fold, val fold. For test: fit on full fold train, transform test.
Train XGBoost with GPU on each fold, save OOF predictions and test fold predictions. Repeat with multiple seeds.
Train LightGBM with GPU on each fold, save OOF predictions and test fold predictions. Repeat with multiple seeds.
Train CatBoost with GPU on each fold (pass cat_features), save OOF predictions and test fold predictions. Repeat with multiple seeds.
Average predictions across seeds for each model. Compute per-model CV AUC.
Rank-transform OOF predictions from all three models, fit Ridge meta-learner, apply to rank-transformed test predictions.
Generate submission.csv with blended predictions. Save all OOF arrays for cross-candidate ensembling.
Save the full pipeline as submit.py
0.design_notes: Estimated training time: XGBoost GPU on 630K rows with ~100+ features should train in under 1 minute per 100 rounds. With early stopping around 500-2000 rounds, expect ~5-10 minutes per fold. LightGBM GPU similar. CatBoost slightly slower on GPU but comparable. Total for 3 models x K folds x 3 seeds: roughly 45-90 minutes for 5 folds. This is the fastest candidate and should serve as a strong baseline. GBDTs excel on tabular data with engineered features. The digit extraction trick specifically exploits synthetic data generation patterns in Playground Series datasets. Risk: GBDTs alone may plateau around AUC 0.952-0.954 without neural models in the ensemble.

0.name: RealMLP with Knowledge Distillation (Teacher-Student)
0.architecture: Two-stage RealMLP pipeline using pytabkit's RealMLP_TD_Classifier. Stage 1 (Teacher): Train a RealMLP with tuned parameters (act='mish', block_str='w-b-a-d' for weight-batchnorm-activation-dropout blocks, weight_param='ntk' for NTK parameterization, use_parametric_act=True, add_front_scale=True). Stage 2 (Student): Merge teacher's OOF predictions as an additional feature ('teacher_pred'), then train a second RealMLP on the augmented feature set. The student learns from both the original features and the teacher's soft predictions, providing a knowledge distillation boost. Multiple seeds for both teacher and student for stability. Final predictions come from the multi-seed student ensemble.
0.data_pipeline: 1) Load train.csv and test.csv. Map target: Absence=0, Presence=1. Drop 'id'. 2) DIGIT FEATURES: Same digit extraction as Candidate 1 -- extract integer digits (pos_1, pos_2, ...) and decimal digits (dec_1, dec_2) from all numeric columns (Age, BP, Cholesterol, Max HR, ST depression). 3) BINNING FEATURES: Quantile bins (q=4,5,10,20) and uniform bins (bins=5,10) for Age, BP, Cholesterol, Max HR, ST depression. Age rounded to nearest 5. 4) CATEGORICAL COPIES: Convert every base feature to string/category type for a categorical version. 5) TARGET ENCODING: Using sklearn.preprocessing.TargetEncoder within CV folds on all categorical features. 6) RealMLP handles some preprocessing internally (numerical normalization), but provide all engineered features as a pandas DataFrame with proper dtype annotation. Mark categorical columns explicitly via cat_col_names parameter. 7) For teacher training: use the full engineered feature set. 8) For student training: add teacher OOF prediction as an additional numeric column named 'teacher_pred'.
0.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy (internal to RealMLP_TD_Classifier). Set use_ls=False to disable label smoothing since the target metric is AUC, not calibrated probabilities. Configure ls_eps_sched='coslog4' for the label smoothing epsilon schedule (even with use_ls=False, this parameter affects internal scheduling). The knowledge distillation is implicit: the teacher_pred feature carries soft probability information that the student can leverage without a separate distillation loss.
0.regularization: RealMLP built-in regularization: p_drop_sched='flat_cos' (dropout schedule that starts flat and decays with cosine), wd_sched='flat_cos' (weight decay schedule). Tune p_drop (base dropout rate) and wd (base weight decay). use_parametric_act=True adds learnable activation function parameters. weight_param='ntk' uses Neural Tangent Kernel parameterization which provides implicit regularization through weight scaling. add_front_scale=True adds a learnable scaling layer at the input. Multiple seeds provide ensemble regularization.
0.training_config: Optimizer: RealMLP's internal optimizer (AdamW variant with custom scheduling). Set hidden_sizes to a rectangular architecture (e.g., same width for all hidden layers -- tune the width and number of layers via hidden_sizes parameter as a list). Set n_cv=1 and n_refit=0 to handle CV externally. Use val_fraction for internal train-val split within each CV fold (or pass X_val, y_val explicitly). Set device='cuda'. Train with multiple seeds (at least 3-5 seeds). Set verbosity for logging. Use val_metric_name='cross_entropy' for early stopping. Set n_epochs and batch_size appropriately for the dataset size. For teacher: train on each CV fold, collect OOF predictions. For student: add teacher_pred column, train on each CV fold with same splits. Average predictions across seeds.
0.validation_strategy: StratifiedKFold cross-validation. Primary metric: AUC. For teacher model: compute OOF predictions across all folds, report teacher CV AUC. For student model: compute OOF predictions across all folds, report student CV AUC. Compare teacher vs student AUC to verify distillation benefit. Save both teacher and student OOF arrays for cross-candidate ensembling. Track per-fold AUC with mean +/- std.
method_steps: Load and preprocess data: read CSVs, map target to 0/1, drop id
Implement full feature engineering pipeline: digit extraction, binning, categorical copies (same as Candidate 1)
Implement target encoding within CV folds using sklearn TargetEncoder
Prepare feature DataFrame with proper dtype annotations (numeric vs categorical columns)
Set up StratifiedKFold CV loop

TEACHER STAGE: For each fold, instantiate RealMLP_TD_Classifier with teacher config (act='mish', block_str='w-b-a-d', weight_param='ntk', use_parametric_act=True, add_front_scale=True, ls_eps_sched='coslog4', p_drop_sched='flat_cos', wd_sched='flat_cos', use_ls=False, device='cuda'). Fit on train fold, predict on val fold (OOF) and test. Repeat for multiple seeds.
Collect teacher OOF predictions (average across seeds). Compute teacher CV AUC.

STUDENT STAGE: Add 'teacher_pred' column (teacher OOF for train, teacher test predictions for test). For each fold, train a new RealMLP_TD_Classifier with same config on augmented features. Fit on train fold, predict on val fold (OOF) and test. Repeat for multiple seeds.
Collect student OOF predictions (average across seeds). Compute student CV AUC.
Average test predictions across folds and seeds for final submission.
Save all OOF arrays (teacher and student) to disk for cross-candidate ensembling.
Generate submission.csv. Save pipeline as submit.py.
0.design_notes: Estimated training time: RealMLP on 630K rows with ~100+ features on A100 GPU. Based on pytabkit benchmarks, training should take roughly 2-5 minutes per fold per seed for the teacher, and similar for the student. With 5 folds x 3 seeds x 2 stages = 30 training runs, total estimated time: 60-150 minutes. This is the medium-cost candidate. RealMLP was the top-performing single model family in the 1st place solution. The knowledge distillation trick provides ~0.001-0.002 AUC boost per the competition writeup. Key risk: RealMLP training time on this many features could be longer than estimated if hidden_sizes are too large. Start with moderate architecture sizes and adjust. The 'mish' activation with NTK parameterization and cosine schedules are specifically recommended by the competition winners.

0.name: TabM Neural Ensemble with PLR Embeddings
0.architecture: TabM (Tabular Deep Learning with Multiple predictions) from the tabm library (ICLR 2025). TabM efficiently represents an ensemble of k MLPs with weight sharing, providing parameter-efficient ensembling. Use the 'tabm' architecture type via make_tabm_backbone. Configure with Piecewise Linear Representation (PLR) embeddings from rtdl_num_embeddings for numeric features, which learn adaptive binning of continuous features. The model outputs k predictions per input, which are averaged for the final prediction. A linear head maps from the backbone output to a single logit. Use batch normalization within blocks and the GELU activation function.
0.data_pipeline: 1) Load train.csv and test.csv. Map target: Absence=0, Presence=1. Drop 'id'. 2) DIGIT FEATURES: Same digit extraction as other candidates -- extract integer and decimal digits from numeric columns. 3) BINNING FEATURES: Quantile bins and uniform bins for key continuous features. 4) CATEGORICAL COPIES: Not used for TabM -- instead, use one-hot encoding for low-cardinality categoricals (Sex, Chest pain type, FBS over 120, EKG results, Exercise angina, Slope of ST, Number of vessels fluro, Thallium). 5) TARGET ENCODING: Using sklearn TargetEncoder on categorical features within CV folds, producing numeric versions. 6) Standardization: Apply StandardScaler (fit on train fold, transform val and test) to all numeric features. 7) Separate features into x_num (all numeric features after scaling) and x_cat (original categorical columns for one-hot encoding within TabM). 8) Convert to PyTorch tensors for GPU training.
0.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy loss (torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss). The dataset is roughly balanced (~45% positive), so no class weighting is needed. The model outputs raw logits; apply sigmoid for probability predictions. Monitor AUC as the primary validation metric for early stopping and model selection.
0.regularization: Dropout within each MLP block of TabM (tune the dropout rate). Weight decay in the optimizer. The TabM architecture itself provides implicit regularization through weight sharing across the k ensemble members. PLR embeddings add regularization by discretizing continuous features into learned bins. Use early stopping based on validation AUC. Optionally use gradient clipping to stabilize training.
0.training_config: Optimizer: AdamW with tuned learning rate and weight decay. Learning rate schedule: CosineAnnealingLR or CosineAnnealingWarmRestarts. Batch size tuned for GPU memory efficiency on A100. Mixed precision training (torch.amp) for speed. Train for a maximum number of epochs with early stopping based on validation AUC (patience parameter). Use k ensemble members in TabM (tune k). Use TabM architecture type 'tabm' with ScalingRandomInitialization for the start_scaling_init. PLR embeddings configured via rtdl_num_embeddings.PiecewiseLinearEncoding with tuned number of bins and sigma. Train with multiple seeds and average predictions. Checkpoint the best model per fold based on validation AUC.
0.validation_strategy: StratifiedKFold cross-validation. Primary metric: AUC (sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score). For each fold, track training loss, validation loss, and validation AUC per epoch. Use early stopping on validation AUC. Save OOF predictions and per-fold test predictions. Average test predictions across folds and seeds. Save all OOF arrays for cross-candidate ensembling. Report per-fold AUC and mean +/- std.
method_steps: Load and preprocess data: read CSVs, map target to 0/1, drop id
Implement feature engineering pipeline: digit extraction, binning (same approach as other candidates)
Implement target encoding within CV folds using sklearn TargetEncoder
Standardize numeric features (fit on train fold, transform val and test)
Prepare one-hot encoding specifications for categorical features
Set up StratifiedKFold CV loop

For each fold: build TabM model using make_tabm_backbone with tuned n_blocks, d_block, dropout, k, and 'tabm' arch_type. Add PLR embeddings via rtdl_num_embeddings.PiecewiseLinearEncoding for numeric features. Add linear output head (d_block -> 1).
Implement training loop: AdamW optimizer, CosineAnnealingLR scheduler, BCEWithLogitsLoss, mixed precision with torch.amp. Train with early stopping on validation AUC.
Save best checkpoint per fold. Collect OOF predictions and test predictions.
Repeat for multiple seeds. Average predictions across seeds.
Compute CV AUC. Save OOF arrays for cross-candidate ensembling.
Generate submission.csv. Save pipeline as submit.py.
0.design_notes: Estimated training time: TabM with PLR embeddings on 630K rows on A100. With moderate architecture (tuned depth, width, and k), expect roughly 10-30 seconds per epoch depending on batch size and feature count. With early stopping around 50-200 epochs, each fold should take 10-30 minutes. With 5 folds x 3 seeds = 15 runs, total: 150-450 minutes. This is the heaviest candidate. TabM (ICLR 2025) represents the state-of-the-art in tabular deep learning with parameter-efficient ensembling. PLR embeddings help capture non-linear feature relationships. Key risk: training time may exceed budget if architecture is too large -- start with conservative sizes. The weight-sharing in TabM provides built-in ensembling that complements the external multi-seed ensembling. TabM predictions should be diverse from both GBDTs and RealMLP, making it valuable for the final cross-candidate ensemble.
